summary: Gruber argues that Apple Intelligence represents the correct strategic approach to generative AI: treating it as features integrated into existing products rather than standalone chatbot products. He draws a parallel to Steve Jobs telling Dropbox it was 'a feature, not a product,' suggesting LLMs are similarly best deployed as enhancements to existing apps. Apple built its own foundation models with no chatbot, instead weaving AI into Safari, Mail, Photos, Siri, and developer frameworks. Gruber notes Apple's focus on personal context via an on-device semantic index and Private Cloud Compute represents unique differentiation. He remains skeptical of the OpenAI partnership's value and cautions that none of these features are yet available to test, making all assessments provisional.
key_points: Apple treated generative AI as features for existing products rather than building a chatbot, paralleling Jobs's 'feature, not a product' critique of Dropbox
Apple Intelligence uses an on-device semantic index for personal context and Private Cloud Compute for complex tasks, all running on Apple's own silicon and software stack
The OpenAI/ChatGPT integration is optional and anonymized, but Gruber suspects it's as much a services-revenue play as a user-experience one and may have undermined Apple's messaging
Device requirements (A17 Pro or M-series minimum) are driven by performance thresholds and cloud scaling concerns, not artificial gatekeeping
None of the Apple Intelligence features are available even in beta yet, making all assessments speculative
money_quotes: Leading up to WWDC last week, I'd been thinking that this same description applies, in spades, to LLM generative AI. Fantastically useful, downright amazing at times, but features. Not products.
Chatbots can be useful. There are people doing amazing work through them. But they're akin to the terminal and command-line tools. Most people just don't think like that.
Apple is using generative AI to make them better and more useful — without, in any way, rendering them unfamiliar.

Apple is focusing on what it can do that no one else can on Apple devices, and not really even trying to compete against ChatGPT et al. for world-knowledge context. They're focusing on unique differentiation, and eschewing commoditization.
Anyone can make a chatbot. And, seemingly, everyone is — searching for 'chatbot' in the App Store is about as useful as searching for 'game.' Apple, conspicuously, has not made one.
AI-generated slop is polluting web search results for technical help; Apple is using targeted AI trained on its own documentation to avoid the need to search the web in the first place.
It all looks very impressive, and almost all these features seem very practical. But it's all very, very early. None of it counts as real until we're able to use it ourselves.

If generative AI weren't seen as essential — both in terms of consumer marketing and investor confidence — I think much, if not most, of what Apple unveiled in 'Apple Intelligence' wouldn't even have been announced until next year's WWDC.
key_insight: Apple's strategic genius with Apple Intelligence is treating generative AI as invisible infrastructure woven into familiar products rather than competing in the commoditized chatbot market, though the proof remains entirely in future execution.
